narrative:
Jimmy Gomez represents California's 34th District—downtown Los Angeles, Boyle Heights, Eagle Rock, Chinatown—a stretch of the city where the median home price hovers around $1 million and the people who actually live there mostly rent [1][2]. A former union organizer and state legislator, Gomez won a 2017 special election and has built a brand as a progressive who does things: founding the Congressional Renters Caucus, launching the Congressional Dads Caucus, and authoring California's landmark paid family leave expansion that President Obama held up as a national model [3][4]. He sits on the House Ways and Means Committee, which is where tax policy lives and dies, and he has used that perch aggressively.

Gomez's alignment with the 80% Bill is substantial and, on several items, backed by actual legislation rather than just press releases. He co-authored a bill with Bernie Sanders to close estate tax loopholes—the For the 99.5% Act—that would raise nearly $550 billion over a decade by ensuring billionaires cannot pass fortunes to their heirs tax-free [5]. He sponsored legislation to ban members of Congress from buying or selling stock and helped pass what his office calls the most comprehensive anti-corruption bill since Watergate [3]. On drug pricing, he is a founding member of the Medicare for All Caucus [6]. On junk fees, he co-introduced the End Junk Fees for Renters Act with Maxwell Frost to ban predatory application fees, screening fees, and late-fee profiteering by landlords [7][8]. On housing, he introduced the Make Housing Affordable and Defend Democracy Act, which would redirect $175 billion toward renter support, down-payment assistance, and converting vacant offices to housing [9].

But follow the money and the progressive sheen develops some interesting cracks. Since taking office, Gomez has accepted approximately $2.7 million from AIPAC, according to AIPAC Tracker [10]. In his 2024 race, AIPAC's super PAC spent over $1.7 million supporting him and another $576,454 attacking his progressive challenger David Kim [11]. The cryptocurrency lobby was the second-largest outside spender in that race, with the Fairshake PAC dropping $511,000 on his behalf [12]. Beyond the super PACs, his top career donors include real estate groups, insurance companies, law firms, and securities and investment outfits [13]—precisely the industries that a renters' advocate and tax reformer might be expected to keep at arm's length.

This creates an awkward geometry. Gomez co-sponsors anti-corruption legislation and talks about banning dark money, then benefits from one of the most aggressive super PAC operations in American politics. He fights for renters while cashing checks from real estate interests. He pushes to tax billionaires while the crypto lobby—an industry built in part on helping the wealthy avoid traditional financial oversight—spends half a million dollars to keep him in office [12]. His 2026 primary challenger, Angela Gonzales-Torres, is running explicitly on rejecting corporate PAC money, which tells you something about how Gomez's funding sources play in a district full of the working-class renters he claims to champion [14][15]. Gomez's legislative record on the 80% Bill's provisions is genuinely strong. The question is whether you can credibly fight to get money out of politics while being one of the clearest examples of money in politics.

Jesus "Chuy" Garcia came to the United States from Durango, Mexico as a child, grew up on Chicago's Southwest Side, and built a political career the old-fashioned way — by organizing against the machine before eventually learning to operate within it. A former alderman, state senator, and Cook County commissioner, Garcia first caught national attention in 2015 when he forced Rahm Emanuel into a historic mayoral runoff, running as the progressive challenger against the Democratic establishment's anointed prince [1]. He won his seat representing Illinois's 4th Congressional District — the earmuff-shaped district designed to connect two Latino neighborhoods on Chicago's North and South sides — in 2018, and quickly established himself as one of the most progressive members of the House [2].

Garcia's alignment with the 80% Bill's platform is among the strongest in the Illinois delegation. He has been a vocal supporter of taxing the ultra-wealthy, cosponsoring legislation to impose a wealth tax and repeatedly calling for billionaires to pay their fair share [3]. He backed Medicare drug price negotiation, voted for paid family leave provisions, and has championed funding Social Security by lifting the payroll tax cap. On campaign finance, Garcia has been a cosponsor of the DISCLOSE Act and has supported measures to overturn Citizens United, consistent with his long-stated view that corporate money corrodes democracy [4]. He voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF and has been broadly supportive of reining in unchecked executive war powers [5].

On policing and transparency, Garcia — representing a district where encounters between law enforcement and communities of color are a daily concern — has been a strong advocate for police body cameras and broader accountability measures. He cosponsored the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act and has pushed for federal oversight of police departments [6]. He has also backed efforts to ban junk fees, supported right-to-repair legislation, and endorsed fair elections reforms including anti-gerrymandering measures — a somewhat ironic position given that his own district is one of the most creatively gerrymandered in the nation, though it was drawn to ensure Latino representation under the Voting Rights Act rather than for partisan advantage [7].

Where Garcia's progressive credentials meet the fundraising realities of Congress, the picture grows more complicated but remains relatively clean. His campaigns have relied more heavily on small-dollar and individual contributions than many of his colleagues, and his top donors have included labor unions, advocacy organizations, and progressive PACs rather than the corporate interests that dominate many members' coffers [8]. He has not been a significant recipient of financial services or fossil fuel money. That said, Garcia has not formally sworn off all corporate PAC money with the fervor of the Squad members he often votes alongside, leaving a sliver of daylight between his rhetoric and his fundraising practice [9].

Garcia is, in sum, one of the House members whose record most closely tracks what the 80% Bill demands. His biography — immigrant, organizer, machine challenger — gives him an authenticity on populist economics and anti-corruption reform that few colleagues can match. If the 80% Bill is a test of whether a member of Congress actually believes in the reforms that supermajorities of Americans support, Chuy Garcia passes with a higher grade than most, even if the extra credit assignments on term limits and the lifetime lobbying ban remain conspicuously unfinished [10].

Rep. Sylvia Garcia has represented Houston's TX-29 since 2019, making history as the first Hispanic member of the Houston Congressional Delegation. She serves as Vice Ranking Member of the House Financial Services Committee, a position that gives her front-row access to the levers of Wall Street regulation. It's a powerful seat — and one that her donors have clearly noticed.

Nearly 77% of Garcia's campaign funding in the 2023-2024 cycle came from PAC contributions, with oil and gas interests chipping in $80,000 and AIPAC adding another $22,333. For a Democrat who built her brand as Houston's "taxpayers' watchdog" during her tenure as City Controller, that's a remarkable amount of corporate cash flowing into a campaign that raised less than 1% from small individual donors. The watchdog, it seems, has been domesticated.

On the Financial Services Committee, Garcia has championed digital asset working groups and affordable housing legislation. What she has not championed is a ban on congressional stock trading — a curious omission for someone sitting on the committee that literally oversees the financial markets. When asked about term limits, Garcia has said she believes "the best form of term limits is the power of the vote," which is the kind of answer that sounds principled until you realize she's been in elected office continuously since the 1990s.

Garcia served as an impeachment manager during Trump's first impeachment trial in 2020 — a genuinely historic role and one she deserves credit for. But courage on impeachment doesn't translate to courage on the 80% Bill's core reforms. On the DISCLOSE Act, dark money bans, corporate PAC bans, and lobbying restrictions, her record is one of comfortable Democratic orthodoxy: say the right things in press releases, then quietly accept the same fundraising structures you claim to oppose.

On Medicare drug negotiation and paid family leave, Garcia generally votes with her caucus. But "voting with your caucus" is not leadership — it's attendance. On Pentagon audits, protecting farmland from corporate buyers, banning junk fees, and the right to repair, Garcia has not distinguished herself with any meaningful legislative action.

The 80% Bill's 20 policies enjoy overwhelming bipartisan support from American voters, including the working-class Houston communities Garcia represents. Her constituents in the East End, Pasadena, and Jacinto City aren't asking for digital asset working groups. They want someone who will fight to ban the corporate money pipeline that turns their representative into a fundraising operation with a congressional office attached.

Lance Gooden, the Republican congressman from Texas's 5th district, is perhaps best known for the one time he accidentally voted for something useful. In March 2021, Gooden became the sole Republican to vote for the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act — then immediately tweeted that he'd "accidentally pressed the wrong voting button." The official record was changed to reflect his opposition. It was the most honest moment of his career: the one time he sided with police accountability, it was literally a mistake.

Before entering Congress in 2019, Gooden spent a decade in the insurance industry, which may explain his comfort on the House Financial Services Committee — the panel that oversees the very industries that once employed him and now fund him. His career has been a masterclass in serving the financial sector while dressing it up as populism. His latest crusade? Proposing cryptocurrency ATMs in federal government buildings, writing to the GSA administrator that such machines would serve as an "educational resource" and signal that "the government is embracing innovation." When your idea of public service is installing crypto kiosks in post offices, your priorities have drifted somewhat from the concerns of working families in East Texas.

Gooden's relationship with January 6th deserves its own chapter. He was billed as a featured speaker at Ali Alexander's "Wild Protest" outside the Capitol on that day — the same Ali Alexander later investigated by the January 6th Committee for organizing the rally that preceded the attack. Hours after the insurrection, Gooden voted to object to certifying the electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania, joining the effort to overturn a democratic election. He then voted against establishing a House committee to investigate what happened. If you're going to help plan the party, it makes sense to vote against investigating the cleanup.

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Gooden is a reliable no-show. He hasn't co-sponsored stock trading bans for Congress, hasn't championed the DISCLOSE Act, and hasn't pushed for term limits despite being exactly the kind of career-track politician the policy targets. He opposed a central bank digital currency while simultaneously lobbying to put crypto ATMs in government lobbies — a position that makes sense only if your goal is to protect private crypto interests over public financial infrastructure. Body cameras, paid family leave, junk fee bans, Pentagon audits — on each of these broadly popular reforms, Gooden is either silent or opposed.

The former insurance broker turned Financial Services Committee member has found his niche: converting committee access into industry-friendly legislation while keeping his head down on the reforms that 80% of Americans actually want. Texas's 5th district deserves a representative who presses the right buttons on purpose.

Michael Guest is a former district attorney from Madison County who has represented Mississippi's 3rd District since 2019, bringing a prosecutor's instinct for knowing exactly when to look the other way [1]. He chairs the House Ethics Committee and serves on Appropriations, a combination that gives him unusual power over both the federal purse strings and the rules governing how his colleagues behave while spending from them [2]. He is also, as it happens, a man who has repeatedly violated the very stock trading disclosure laws his committee is supposed to enforce.

Guest voted against the Inflation Reduction Act in August 2022, which included the Medicare Drug Negotiation program allowing the government to negotiate lower prescription drug prices for seniors, calling it a path to "higher taxes and more inflation" [3]. He voted against certifying the 2020 electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6-7, 2021, then turned around and voted with 35 other Republicans to establish a bipartisan commission to investigate the Capitol attack, a move that nearly cost him his seat in the 2022 Republican primary [4][5]. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money in politics, ban corporate PACs, impose a lifetime lobbying ban, enact paid family leave, or audit the Pentagon. His AFL-CIO score hovered around 9-10% in 2023 and 2024, indicating consistent opposition to the working-family priorities that anchor the 80% Bill [6].

The stock trading question deserves its own paragraph, because Guest has made it personal. In 2021, he violated the STOCK Act by filing disclosure of an Exxon purchase and BP sale eight months late, paying the statutory $200 fine, which amounts to a parking ticket for insider trading compliance [7]. Then in 2023, he traded an estimated $500,000 in stocks and options, including Uranium Energy Corporation, Arista Networks, and International Seaways, again without the timely disclosure required by law [8]. As recently as January 2026, he sold Chord Energy stock [9]. This is the chairman of the Ethics Committee, the man who oversees congressional conduct, actively trading energy stocks while sitting on the Appropriations subcommittees for Energy and Water Development and Military Construction [10]. When the 80% Bill proposes banning congressional stock trading, it is describing something Guest does regularly and discloses reluctantly.

Guest's most visible moment came when the Ethics Committee investigated George Santos, ultimately leading Guest to introduce the expulsion resolution in November 2023, which passed 311-114 [11]. But when the committee's report on Matt Gaetz, which found evidence of payments for sex and drugs, came up for release in December 2024, Guest dissented, arguing the committee had no jurisdiction over former members, a position that conveniently aligned with the preferences of the incoming Trump administration that had nominated Gaetz for Attorney General [12][13]. The selective application of ethical standards is, in its way, a form of expertise.

In the 2024 cycle, Guest raised approximately $767,000 and ran unopposed in the general election [14]. He has built a career on the kind of quiet power that does not require defending itself to voters. The 80% Bill asks whether members of Congress should be banned from trading stocks, forced to disclose their donors, and required to negotiate drug prices for Medicare. Guest's answer, delivered not in words but in trades, votes, and silences, is a resounding no.

Jared Golden is the Democratic congressman representing Maine's 2nd District, an enormous rural expanse stretching from Bangor to the Canadian border where lobster traps outnumber lobbyists and "bipartisan" is not a dirty word. A Marine Corps veteran who served two combat tours in Iraq and Afghanistan, Golden flipped the district in 2018 and has held it since by cultivating a brand of muscular independence -- voting against his own party more than any other House Democrat in 2023 [1]. He sits on the Armed Services Committee and the Small Business Committee, assignments that track with both his military background and his district's economic reality.

On the 80% bills, Golden is one of the more interesting cases in Congress: a Democrat who actually leads on several of these issues rather than merely paying lip service. He has been the driving force behind bipartisan efforts to ban congressional stock trading, co-leading coalitions with Republican Brian Fitzpatrick in both 2022 and 2024 demanding floor votes on legislation that would prohibit members from owning or trading individual stocks [2]. He introduced the Ban Corporate PACs Act to eliminate corporate political action committees entirely, and his Crack Down on Dark Money Act would cap 501(c)(4) political spending at 10 percent of total expenditures and force disclosure of large donors [3]. He voted to release the Epstein files [4] and used his Small Business subcommittee chairmanship to push right-to-repair legislation, hauling an Aroostook County farmer before Congress to testify about manufacturers blocking equipment repairs [5].

Where Golden gets complicated is on the issues where his independence shades into contrarianism. He was the only Democrat to vote against the Bipartisan Background Checks Act, voted against the assault weapons ban, and most recently sided with Republicans in March 2026 to reject a war powers resolution that would have constrained military operations in Iran [6]. These votes make him a hero to some Mainers and a headache for Democratic leadership, but they also mean his record on the 80% bills is uneven -- strong on government transparency and anti-corruption, murkier on items where he breaks from the populist consensus to stake out hawkish or gun-friendly positions.

On campaign finance, Golden took a "no corporate PAC" pledge in 2018 and has largely kept it -- his campaign does not accept money from PACs established and controlled by corporations, though it does accept trade association PAC money, a distinction his critics call a loophole [7]. He held a substantial $3.3 million war chest heading into 2024 and raised $4.8 million for the cycle, with Jane Street Capital topping his joint fundraising committee donors at $21,600 [8]. The money is not as eye-popping as what flows to senators, but the trade association carve-out is worth noting for a man who built his brand on fighting corporate influence.

The bottom line on Jared Golden is that he is the rare member of Congress who actually champions several 80% bills -- stock trading bans, corporate PAC bans, dark money disclosure, Epstein transparency, right to repair -- and does so across party lines. His independent streak is genuine, not performed, which means he sometimes lands on the wrong side of popular opinion too. But in a Congress full of representatives who talk about reform and never file a bill, Golden files the bills. Whether he can drag his colleagues to a vote is another question entirely.

Carlos Gimenez arrived in Miami from Havana at the age of six, fleeing Castro's Cuba with his family in 1960 [1]. He spent twenty-five years as a firefighter-paramedic, rising to become Miami's first Cuban-American fire chief, then served as City Manager of Miami before winning the Miami-Dade County mayoral seat in a 2011 special election [1]. He was elected to Congress in 2020, representing what is now Florida's 28th district, and currently sits on the House Armed Services Committee — the first Miami-Dade member on that panel in modern history — as well as the Homeland Security Committee and the Select Committee on Strategic Competition with the Chinese Communist Party [2][3]. Gimenez is, in short, the kind of Republican who can credibly claim both a bootstrap immigrant story and deep roots in South Florida's political establishment.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Gimenez offers a split personality that is instructive about the modern GOP. He co-led the bipartisan Nunn-McCurdy Reform Act of 2025 with Democrat John Garamendi, a bill designed to crack down on Pentagon cost overruns and improve congressional oversight of defense acquisition programs — a position that aligns neatly with the 80% Bill's call to audit the Pentagon [4]. As Miami-Dade mayor, he championed term limits for county commissioners, which voters approved by a 77-to-23 margin in 2012, suggesting at least a historical sympathy for the term-limits plank [5]. He also voted in favor of the Epstein Files Transparency Act when it reached the House floor, though not before initially downplaying the issue by telling CNN that "Americans are over the Epstein case" — a comment that aged poorly once the files actually started dropping [6]. On January 6, he voted to object to electoral certification but then voted in favor of a bipartisan commission to investigate the insurrection, a straddle that captures his political positioning rather precisely [7].

Where Gimenez departs from the 80% Bill consensus is on virtually every economic populist plank. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — a provision supported by roughly ninety percent of Americans in polls [8]. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, close the carried interest loophole, tax the ultra-wealthy, establish paid family leave, or ban corporate PACs. On campaign finance transparency and the DISCLOSE Act, his record is silent in the affirmative. He co-sponsored the Affordable Housing Credit Improvement Act of 2023 and the PRICE Act, gestures toward the housing crisis, but has not backed the more aggressive approach of banning corporate purchases of single-family homes that the 80% Bill envisions [9].

The donor ledger tells the familiar story. During his 2020 congressional run, more than $170,000 came from developers and builders — over a third of his total fundraising [10]. Billionaire developer Jorge Pérez gave the maximum $5,800, alongside GL Homes chair Itzhak Ezratti and Century Homebuilder CEO Sergio Pino [10]. The real estate and construction sector has remained a dominant funding source across his congressional campaigns, which creates an obvious tension with any legislation that would restrict corporate or institutional homebuying [10]. His Armed Services Committee seat, meanwhile, places him at the nexus of defense spending decisions while the Pentagon continues to fail its annual audit — a dynamic the Nunn-McCurdy bill attempts to address, though critics note it targets procurement inefficiency rather than the structural overspending that audit reformers have in mind.

Gimenez is a Republican who occasionally crosses party lines on procedural and transparency issues but reliably holds the line on the economic questions where corporate donors have the most at stake. He will co-lead a bill to punish Pentagon waste, but he will not vote to let Medicare negotiate drug prices. He will support term limits in theory, but he will not touch the carried interest loophole or dark money in practice. For the 80% Bill scorecard, that pattern produces a predictable result: partial credit on government accountability, near-zero on the kitchen-table economics that most Americans say they actually care about.

Tony Gonzales, the soon-to-be-former congressman from Texas's 23rd district, managed to accomplish something truly rare in modern politics: getting censured by his own party while simultaneously failing to impress anyone else. The Navy veteran turned Appropriations Committee member built a brand as a "moderate Republican" — which in practice meant voting for the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act after the Uvalde massacre in his own district, then watching the Texas GOP censure him for it alongside his votes on same-sex marriage protections. Principles are great until your state party chairman is drafting a resolution against you.

But let's not confuse occasional bipartisan gestures with actual reform. Gonzales's top donor was AIPAC, which funneled nearly $300,000 into his coffers during the 2024 cycle, while the defense industry contributed over $600,000. As a member of the Appropriations Committee — the panel that literally decides where taxpayer money goes — Gonzales dutifully secured millions for defense contractors operating in his district, including $4 million for trainer jet transitions at Laughlin Air Force Base and $72 million for rare earth supply chain projects. When your committee controls the checkbook and your donors build the weapons, "supporting the troops" starts looking a lot like supporting the quarterly earnings reports.

On the 80% Bill's priorities, Gonzales was largely absent. He never co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, never pushed for the DISCLOSE Act, and never championed Pentagon audits despite sitting on the committee best positioned to demand them. Term limits? He had no stated position — though fate intervened when, in March 2026, he dropped his reelection bid after admitting to an affair with a congressional aide who later died by suicide. The House Ethics Committee had opened an investigation, and Speaker Johnson reportedly urged him to step aside. It turns out the term limit Tony Gonzales needed was the one imposed by his own conduct.

His campaign raised over $4.3 million in the 2024 cycle, with only 6% coming from small donors — the people he was ostensibly representing. The rest flowed from PACs and large individual contributors, the usual cocktail of defense firms, pro-Israel lobbies, and corporate interests that expect a return on investment. For a district stretching from San Antonio to El Paso, populated by working families and veterans, Gonzales offered the appearance of independence while delivering the substance of business as usual.

Rep. Andrew Garbarino styles himself as Long Island's indispensable moderate -- the kind of Republican who'll vote for same-sex marriage [1] and bipartisan infrastructure [2] just often enough to keep the "independent thinker" brand alive, while cashing checks from the very industries his Financial Services Committee oversees. His top donor sectors read like a Wall Street networking event: Securities & Investment ($514,385), Insurance ($314,164), and Real Estate ($272,806) [3]. Convenient, then, that he sits on the Capital Markets subcommittee as Vice Chairman and the Housing and Insurance subcommittee [4]. The revolving door between his committee gavel and his campaign coffers is less a door than an open archway.

Garbarino signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge [5], which is a nice gesture from a man now serving his third term with no apparent plans to stop. He has taken at least $125,000 from corporations, corporate PACs, and LLCs over his career [6], and has not cosponsored any version of the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act [7] -- perhaps because transparency might complicate things for his financial sector benefactors. He has shown zero interest in the DISCLOSE Act, Pentagon audit reform, or banning dark money, despite representing a district where 80% of voters support these measures.

His crowning moment came on May 22, 2025, when he literally fell asleep and missed the vote on the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act," which passed 215-214 [8]. Speaker Mike Johnson joked he was "going to strangle" Garbarino [9]. The bill -- containing trillions in tax cuts and gutting Medicaid -- squeaked by without his vote. Garbarino later claimed he was "moments away from the House floor" [10], a masterful euphemism for "napping in the back." Trump, ever the forgiving patron, promptly endorsed him anyway, calling him "a Fierce Advocate for Long Island" who "WILL NEVER LET YOU DOWN" [11] -- unless, of course, you need him awake for a vote.

To his credit, Garbarino fought hard to raise the SALT deduction cap from $10,000 to $40,000, co-chairing the bipartisan SALT Caucus [12]. But championing a tax break that disproportionately benefits upper-income homeowners in your district is less "populist hero" and more "basic constituent service for a wealthy suburban seat." On the 80% Bill issues that actually challenge corporate power -- banning corporate PACs, closing the carried interest loophole, mandating police body cameras, establishing paid family leave -- Garbarino is conspicuously silent. He chairs the Homeland Security Committee [13] but has not leveraged that platform for any of the twenty broadly popular reforms. The moderate brand is carefully curated; the courage to challenge his donors is not.

Robert Garcia, Democrat of California's 42nd district, is a sophomore congressman who has already rocketed into House Democratic leadership as the youngest Ranking Member of the Oversight Committee in a century [1]. A former mayor of Long Beach — the city's first Latino and first openly LGBTQ+ mayor — Garcia arrived in Washington in 2023 with a progressive brand polished to a high sheen and an immigrant story (born in Peru, became a citizen at 21) that practically writes its own campaign ads [2]. He sits on the Oversight and Government Reform Committee, where he now leads the Democratic side, and the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee [3].

On paper, Garcia checks a remarkable number of boxes on the 80% Bill. He co-sponsored the bipartisan TRUST in Congress Act to ban congressional stock trading, declaring that members shouldn't be "making money using insider information" [4]. He pledged to refuse corporate PAC money and says he "strongly believes in ending Citizens United" [5]. He co-sponsored the Social Security 2100 Act to expand and shore up the program, advocates for Medicare for All, and has pressed the Social Security Administration to protect beneficiaries [6][7]. On the Epstein files — a signature 80% Bill issue — Garcia has been arguably the most aggressive member of Congress, using his Oversight perch to demand full transparency, subpoena Epstein associates like Les Wexner, call for Trump to testify under oath, and demand answers from former AG Merrick Garland [8][9][10]. He co-authored a new disclosure bill to strengthen campaign finance transparency and has spoken favorably about Pentagon spending accountability, noting the "ballooning Pentagon budget" [11][12]. He also supported the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act and recently introduced the Stop Militarizing Our Streets Act [13].

Here is where the shine develops some interesting fingerprints. Despite campaigning against super PAC influence, Garcia's 2022 primary saw over $1 million in independent expenditures on his behalf — including $866,522 from Protect Our Future PAC, largely bankrolled by crypto billionaire Sam Bankman-Fried (yes, that Sam Bankman-Fried) [14]. He also received over $500,000 in support from AIPAC's United Democracy Project super PAC, which spent heavily to oppose his primary rival [14][15]. Garcia didn't solicit that money, technically, but one might note that Citizens United is considerably easier to oppose when its infrastructure is working in your favor. His campaign has also benefited from donations from individuals in the real estate and tech sectors, industries that don't always share his constituents' enthusiasm for affordable housing and corporate accountability [16].

Garcia's record on the 80% Bill is genuinely strong compared to many of his colleagues. He hits the right notes on stock trading bans, campaign finance reform, Social Security, police accountability, and the Epstein investigation with unusual vigor. The gap between his rhetoric on dark money and the dark money that boosted his career is the kind of contradiction that makes voters' eyes glaze over — and lobbyists' eyes light up. Still, if you're scoring on actions rather than funding sources, Garcia lands well inside the 80% Bill's strike zone, even if the money trail occasionally wanders outside the lines [17].
